name: Phishing Email
description: Emails impersonating banks or services to steal login credentials and personal information
detailedDescription: Phishing emails are fraudulent messages designed to look like they come from trusted sources such as banks, payment processors, or popular services. They typically contain links to fake websites that capture your login credentials, credit card numbers, or other sensitive information.
exampleIndicators: Generic greeting like 'Dear Customer' instead of your name
Urgent language: 'Your account will be suspended'
Mismatched sender email domain (e.g., support@bank-security-update123.com)
Links that don't match the claimed organization (hover to check)
Requests for password, SSN, or credit card via email
Poor grammar and spelling errors
Threats of account closure if you don't act immediately
howToSpot: Check the sender's actual email address, not just the display name
Hover over links before clicking to see the real URL
Legitimate companies never ask for passwords via email
Look for your name — real companies address you personally
Check for HTTPS and correct domain in any URL

name: USPS/FedEx Smishing
description: Fake package delivery text messages requesting redelivery fees or personal information
detailedDescription: Smishing (SMS phishing) scams impersonate delivery services like USPS, FedEx, UPS, and DHL. They claim a package couldn't be delivered and ask you to click a link to reschedule or pay a small fee. The link leads to a phishing site that steals your personal and payment information.
exampleIndicators: Text from an unknown number about a package you're not expecting
Short links (bit.ly, tinyurl) instead of official USPS/FedEx URLs
Requests for a 'redelivery fee' or 'customs charge'
USPS never sends texts with links — they use tracking numbers only
Misspelled company names or slightly wrong URLs
Pressure to act within hours
howToSpot: Track packages directly on the carrier's official website or app
USPS, FedEx, and UPS never charge redelivery fees via text
Check if you're actually expecting a package
Never click links in unexpected delivery texts
Real delivery notifications include your tracking number

name: IRS Impersonation
description: Fake tax debt or refund messages pretending to be from the IRS or tax authorities
detailedDescription: Scammers impersonate the IRS, state tax agencies, or tax preparation services. They claim you owe back taxes, face arrest, or have a pending refund. They demand immediate payment via gift cards, wire transfer, or cryptocurrency. The real IRS will never initiate contact via phone, email, or text.
exampleIndicators: The IRS initiates contact by postal mail, never by phone, email, or text
Demands for immediate payment via gift cards, wire transfer, or crypto
Threats of arrest, deportation, or license revocation
Requests for credit card or debit card numbers over the phone
Demands that you pay without the opportunity to question or appeal
Claims you'll be arrested if you don't pay right now
howToSpot: The IRS always sends written notices through the mail first
The IRS never demands immediate payment over the phone
The IRS never asks for gift cards, wire transfers, or cryptocurrency
The IRS never threatens to bring in police to arrest you
When in doubt, call the IRS directly at 1-800-829-1040

name: AI Voice Cloning
description: Deepfake voice of a family member in an 'emergency' situation requesting money
detailedDescription: Using AI voice cloning technology, scammers can replicate a family member's voice from just a few seconds of audio found on social media. They then call pretending to be that person in an emergency — in jail, in an accident, kidnapped — and urgently request money for bail, medical bills, or ransom.
exampleIndicators: A call from an unknown number sounding like a loved one in distress
Urgent request for money via wire transfer, gift cards, or crypto
The caller asks you not to hang up or contact anyone else
The call comes at an unusual hour to catch you off guard
Unnatural pauses or robotic cadence in the voice
The person can't answer specific personal questions only they would know
howToSpot: Ask a question only the real person would know the answer to
Hang up and call the person's actual phone number directly
Don't trust caller ID — it can be spoofed
Be suspicious of any request for immediate money transfer
Verify the story with another family member

name: Romance Scam
description: Fake online relationship that builds trust before requesting money
detailedDescription: Romance scammers create fake dating profiles or social media accounts to build emotional relationships with victims over weeks or months. Once trust is established, they fabricate emergencies, business troubles, or travel expenses and ask for money. AI-generated text makes these scams more convincing than ever.
exampleIndicators: Quick profession of love before meeting in person
Always has an excuse for why they can't video chat or meet
Claims to be working overseas (military, oil rig, peacekeeping)
Asks for money for travel, medical emergencies, or customs fees
Requests payment via gift cards, wire transfer, or cryptocurrency
Photos look professional or are stolen from modeling accounts
Story changes or becomes more elaborate over time
howToSpot: Never send money to someone you haven't met in person
Do a reverse image search on their profile photos
Be wary of overly perfect profiles and rapid emotional escalation
Insist on a video call early in the relationship
Tell a trusted friend about the relationship for a reality check

name: Crypto Investment
description: Guaranteed return investment fraud through fake cryptocurrency platforms
detailedDescription: Crypto investment scams promise guaranteed high returns on cryptocurrency investments. They use fake trading platforms, social media ads, and celebrity deepfakes to appear legitimate. Victims can see their 'profits' growing on the fake platform but cannot withdraw funds without paying additional 'fees' or 'taxes.'
exampleIndicators: Guaranteed returns with zero risk
Pressure to invest immediately before the 'opportunity closes'
Celebrity endorsements that are fabricated or deepfaked
Unregistered trading platforms or apps
You can deposit but withdrawals require more money
Social media messages from strangers about crypto opportunities
Complex jargon designed to confuse and impress
howToSpot: No legitimate investment offers guaranteed returns
Verify the platform is registered with financial regulators
Be skeptical of any investment promoted on social media
Never invest based on a message from someone you don't know
If you can't withdraw your money, it's a scam

name: Tech Support Scam
description: Fake pop-ups or calls claiming your computer is infected and requires paid support
detailedDescription: Tech support scammers display fake virus warnings on websites, send fake emails, or call directly claiming to be from Microsoft, Apple, or Google. They say your device is infected and offer to 'fix' it for a fee. They may request remote access to your device, install malware, or charge for unnecessary 'repairs.'
exampleIndicators: Pop-up that says your computer is infected and you must call a number
A phone call from someone claiming to be from Microsoft or Apple
Requests for remote access to your computer
Demands for payment via gift cards or wire transfer
The warning won't let you close your browser normally
Claims your IP address has been flagged for illegal activity
howToSpot: Microsoft, Apple, and Google never make unsolicited support calls
Legitimate virus warnings come from your installed antivirus software, not web pages
Never give remote access to someone who contacted you
Close the browser using Task Manager or Force Quit if a pop-up won't close
Never call phone numbers shown in browser pop-ups

name: Job Offer Scam
description: Fake employment opportunities requiring upfront payment or personal information
detailedDescription: Job offer scams post fake listings on job boards or send unsolicited offers for high-paying remote work. They may ask for personal information for a 'background check,' require you to purchase equipment from their 'vendor,' or send you a fake check to deposit and return a portion as 'setup fees.'
exampleIndicators: Offered a job without an interview or with minimal contact
Salary is much higher than market rate for the role
Asked to pay for equipment, training, or background checks upfront
Sent a check and asked to deposit it and return some funds
Communication only through messaging apps (WhatsApp, Telegram)
Request for SSN, bank info, or copy of ID before you've been hired
The company has no real online presence or reviews
howToSpot: Research the company independently — check their real website
Legitimate employers never ask you to pay to get hired
Never deposit a check from someone you don't know
Be suspicious of unsolicited job offers, especially via text
Verify the recruiter's identity on LinkedIn

name: Lottery/Sweepstakes
description: 'You've won' messages requiring a fee to claim your prize
detailedDescription: Lottery and sweepstakes scams tell you you've won a prize in a contest you never entered. To claim it, you must pay 'taxes,' 'shipping,' 'insurance,' or 'processing fees' upfront. The prize never arrives. These scams come via email, text, phone, and even physical mail.
exampleIndicators: You won a lottery you never entered
Asked to pay fees or taxes before receiving your prize
Request for bank account info to 'deposit your winnings'
The message comes from a free email service (Gmail, Yahoo)
Poor grammar and generic greetings
Pressure to act quickly before the 'claim period' expires
howToSpot: You cannot win a lottery you didn't enter
Real lotteries never ask winners to pay fees upfront
Never give bank details to claim a prize
If it sounds too good to be true, it is
Legitimate sweepstakes don't require purchase or payment to claim

name: Streaming Billing Scam
description: Fake billing update emails impersonating Netflix, Spotify, and other streaming services
detailedDescription: These phishing emails impersonate popular streaming services like Netflix, Hulu, Spotify, and Disney+. They claim your payment failed, your account will be suspended, or you need to update your billing information. The link leads to a fake login page that captures your credentials and credit card details.
exampleIndicators: Email saying your payment failed or account will be suspended
Links that don't go to the actual service's domain
Requests to 'verify' or 'update' payment information via email link
Sender email doesn't match the official service domain
Generic greeting instead of your account name
Threat of immediate account cancellation
howToSpot: Go directly to the service's website or app — don't click email links
Check your account status in the official app
Netflix and other services will never ask for payment info via email link
Verify the sender's email domain carefully
Check your billing status in the actual app or website

name: Family Emergency
description: 'Your relative is in trouble' messages demanding immediate payment
detailedDescription: Family emergency scams use urgency and fear to manipulate victims. A message or call claims a family member is in jail, in the hospital, stranded, or in danger and needs money immediately. AI-generated text and voice cloning make these increasingly convincing. The scammer often asks you not to contact other family members.
exampleIndicators: A message claiming a family member is in jail or the hospital
Urgent request for bail money, medical bills, or travel expenses
Asks you not to tell anyone else in the family
Request for payment via wire transfer, gift cards, or crypto
The person can't answer personal questions correctly
Message comes from an unknown number
howToSpot: Hang up and call the family member's actual phone number directly
Ask a question only the real person would know
Contact another family member to verify the story
Never send money based on a text or call you can't independently verify
Be suspicious of any request for secrecy

name: Identity Theft
description: Requests for SSN, passwords, or personal information via text or email
detailedDescription: Identity theft scams attempt to collect your personal information — Social Security number, date of birth, bank account numbers, passwords — by impersonating banks, government agencies, employers, or utility companies. With AI, these messages are increasingly personalized and difficult to distinguish from legitimate communications.
exampleIndicators: Requests for your Social Security number via text, email, or call
Asks to 'verify your identity' by providing personal details
Claims your bank account has been compromised
Requests for copies of your driver's license or passport
Asks you to confirm your password or PIN
Messages containing partial information about you to seem legitimate
howToSpot: Banks never ask for your password or PIN via email or text
Government agencies don't request SSN via email or text
Never provide personal information in response to unsolicited contact
If concerned, call the organization directly using a verified number
Enable two-factor authentication on all important accounts
